*Name:* Lytes Cary Manor
*Country:* england
*Region:* south west
*County:* Somerset
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 14th-16th century
*Coords:* 51.0358, -2.6677
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lytes_Cary
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Lytes%20Cary%20Manor/@51.0217,-2.6917,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZNUg5PfgpyaHu7Wh6bDG_Id-oJxjFYITOsIBpwN16lnRBjnQiQARTfAOcnc0eS3gS00MXrMaV3c1083WfqH7XBLQVrdYgeapNqJCyHSADa7we6uh5Ry4lf9875Jw3vYjlFN-Led8TNqZ6ODWA=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Lytes Cary is a manor house with associated chapel and gardens near Charlton Mackrell and Somerton in Somerset, England. The property, owned by the National Trust, has parts dating to the 14th century, with other sections dating to the 15th, 16th, 18th, and 20th centuries. "Yet all parts blend to perfection with one another and with the gentle sunny landscape that surrounds them," comments Nikolaus Pevsner. The House is listed as Grade I by English Heritage.

_Listing 1 of 28_

"Yet all parts blend to perfection with one another and with the gentle sunny landscape that surrounds them," comments Nikolaus Pevsner. The House is listed as Grade I by English Heritage.

*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZMyZwB9f_3b9OZtUcqlyGiIDmkC4kMq_Dp0zGXb4YucUDsZIJsVstRwH2VmEtEsAuW_UV8sDG1vqsvPX4fr-aIxXS0IPwT3pM2Sbuw_5wo9K945TV-8TvVU1lnAzqGGondFqWGOoVKuOBfKkUeot5r8Ug=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
A la Ronde is an 18th-century, 16-sided cottage orné near Lympstone, Exmouth, Devon, England, in the ownership of the National Trust. The house was built for two spinster cousins, Jane and Mary Parminter. It is a Grade I listed building, as are the adjacent Point-In-View chapel, school and almshouses, together with a manse, which were also built by the cousins. The gardens are Grade II listed in the National Register of Historic Parks and Gardens.

_Listing 3 of 28_

*Name:* Wenlock Priory
*Country:* england
*Region:* the midlands
*County:* Shropshire
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 52.59739, -2.55506
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wenlock_Priory
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Wenlock%20Priory/@52.595,-2.5556,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/St_Milburga%27s_Priory%2C_Much_Wenlock_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1652560.jpg/330px-St_Milburga%27s_Priory%2C_Much_Wenlock_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1652560.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Wenlock Priory, or St Milburga's Priory, is a ruined 12th-century monastery, located in Much Wenlock, Shropshire, at grid reference SJ625001. Roger de Montgomery re-founded the Priory as a Cluniac house between 1079 and 1082, on the site of an earlier 7th-century monastery. In 1101 bones, believed to be those of Saint Milburga, were discovered beneath the floor of the old church. The relics were ceremoniously translated to the main monastery church.

_Listing 5 of 28_

*Country:* england

*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Croft_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Croft%20Castle/@52.3033,-2.805,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZNg-X2vnP9XgASnfqTX1q5PcfY_asEhbt4NwMFLetkZjH0OFg7ynYsU3sw6djW37ZrXO4KItBWvvUwYzhNfKJw_EG-shNQHN1DILjcaervVKyJYwxDEDDjOlezIs5jWtFhrGrjLeI0EYd_7Gg=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Croft Castle is a country house in the village of Croft, Herefordshire, England. Owned by the Croft family since 1085, the castle and estate passed out of their hands in the 18th century, before being repurchased by the family in 1923. In 1957 it was bequeathed to the National Trust. The castle is a Grade I listed building, and the estate is separately listed as Grade II*. The adjacent Church of St Michael is listed Grade I.

_Listing 7 of 28_

listing.desc: Croft Castle is a country house in the village of Croft, Herefordshire, England. In 1957 it was bequeathed to the National Trust. The castle is a Grade I listed building, and the estate is separately listed as Grade II*. The adjacent Church of St Michael is listed Grade I.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Boscobel House
*Country:* england
*Region:* the midlands
*County:* Shropshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 17th century
*Coords:* 52.6716, -2.2416
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boscobel_House
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Boscobel%20House/@52.6783,-2.355,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZMNUMtmz1MOT75y5LxNxzAbrBfqkFRUqBsPUnpBuM9Fgt_vONvR1HqGLJ7vsHWhmfdm8HNY6Smh94l239TmjXeLOSISIMcwWqA65b1DH1cYrCk7brXhekfOil9tmpPQuVjYU7jgEvq5StoCzg=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Boscobel House is a Grade II* listed building in the parish of Boscobel in Shropshire. It has been, at various times, a farmhouse, a hunting lodge, and a holiday home; but it is most famous for its role in the escape of Charles II after the Battle of Worcester in 1651. Today it is managed by English Heritage.

_Listing 10 of 28_

listing.name: Boscobel House
listing.desc: Boscobel House is a Grade II* listed building in the parish of Boscobel in Shropshire. It has been, at various times, a farmhouse, a hunting lodge, and a holiday home; but it is most famous for its role in the escape of Charles II after the Battle of Worcester in 1651. Today it is managed by English Heritage.

imageAttribution.description:
Boscobel House. This was the hiding place of Charles II when he was fleeing his Cromwellian pursuers in 1651 having lost disastorously at the final battle of the Civil War, at Worcester (September 3rd 1651). Of course he more famously hid a few yards south of here in the 1289763.
The lefthand range of the building seen here dates from that time, having been built around 1632, but the righthand side is considerably more recent. The family that risked their necks in hiding the fugitive king were the Giffards, and their help was not forgotten by Charles, who on his restoration established a pension for them and their successors - which is still paid to this day. The house is now in the care of English Heritage http://www.english-heritage.org.uk/server.php?show=nav.16650

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Nunnington Hall
*Country:* england
*Region:* yorkshire
*County:* Yorkshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 13th-17th century
*Coords:* 54.20682, -0.97374
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nunnington_Hall
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Nunnington%20Hall/@54.1933,-0.9917,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZOc4SvjS0fcIUpv7YUdevxZjlMN_tlPQr8VNKjmv70-zLOPUsopMOluNni0VopW-Jy3Lzr9kA5-z4iwkOa7jOyUrw2yHYe4__9z6ptX7IToEFONij0dWIpAPps48HxMnd0u61b0jATMj42M2UxmyWhQwQ=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Nunnington Hall is a country house situated in the English county of North Yorkshire. The river Rye, which gives its name to the local area, Ryedale, runs past the house, flowing away from the village of Nunnington. A stone bridge over the river separates the grounds of the house from the village. Above, a ridge known as Caulkley's Bank lies between Nunnington and the Vale of York to the south. The Vale of Pickering and the North York Moors lie to the north and east. Nunnington Hall is owned, conserved and managed as a visitor attraction by the National Trust.

_Listing 12 of 28_
